label: delphi-r1-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is UNLAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a reviewing court finds at least a substantial Article XIII D section 6(b) violation — most plausibly by invalidating the increment of the $0.85 surcharge attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond the cost-justified 1.6x marginal-cost differential, and/or by finding the revenue-use structure (55% rebates flowing mainly to non-payers) breaches the proportionality and revenue-use limits of 6(b)(2)-(3). Full invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a substantial partial invalidation, but the proposition's \"in whole or in substantial part\" framing is satisfied by either outcome.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held tiered water rates must track actual cost of service and rejected conservation-incentive pricing untethered from cost as a rate basis — the district's own study admittedly derives $0.85 'in part' from incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation, closely mirroring the defect that doomed the ordinance in Capistrano.","The district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a largely self-proving basis for a 6(b)(2)/6(b)(3) violation, since fee revenue from high-tier payers appears to cross-subsidize other ratepayers' capital improvements rather than recover costs attributable to the payers' own parcels.","Courts apply independent, non-deferential judicial review to Prop 218 fee-validity questions (see Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586; Capistrano), reducing the likelihood a court simply defers to the district's blended cost/incentive rate-study methodology.","Countervailing factor pulling probability below near-certain: the district has genuine cost-of-service evidence (1.6x marginal cost from peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases), and cases like City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water District (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926 uphold tiered rates supported by real cost differentials, so a court may sever and preserve the cost-justified portion of the surcharge and the recycled-water debt-service allocation (30%), yielding partial rather than total invalidation.","The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is unlikely to succeed given Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, which treats parcel-delivered water charges as property-related fees subject to Prop 218, so that alternative theory does little to lower overall exposure."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water District (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Water Code § 375","California Farm Bureau Federation v. State Water Resources Control Board (2011) 51 Cal.4th 421","Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Proposition 26)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court would treat the gap between the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential and the $0.85 surcharge as a meaningful, severable 'incentive premium' (supporting partial invalidation) or would instead find the two roughly coextensive and defer to the district's rate-study judgment on the theory that peaking/drought-reserve costs alone justify a rate near the full differential — the hypothetical does not quantify how far $0.85 actually diverges from a pure 1.6x cost-based rate."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a reviewing court finds at least a substantial Article XIII D section 6(b) violation — most plausibly by invalidating the increment of the $0.85 surcharge attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" beyond the cost-justified 1.6x marginal-cost differential, and/or by finding the revenue-use structure (55% rebates flowing mainly to non-payers) breaches the proportionality and revenue-use limits of 6(b)(2)-(3). Full invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a substantial partial invalidation, but the proposition's "in whole or in substantial part" framing is satisfied by either outcome.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held tiered water rates must track actual cost of service and rejected conservation-incentive pricing untethered from cost as a rate basis — the district's own study admittedly derives $0.85 'in part' from incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation, closely mirroring the defect that doomed the ordinance in Capistrano.

The district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a largely self-proving basis for a 6(b)(2)/6(b)(3) violation, since fee revenue from high-tier payers appears to cross-subsidize other ratepayers' capital improvements rather than recover costs attributable to the payers' own parcels.

Courts apply independent, non-deferential judicial review to Prop 218 fee-validity questions (see Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586; Capistrano), reducing the likelihood a court simply defers to the district's blended cost/incentive rate-study methodology.

Countervailing factor pulling probability below near-certain: the district has genuine cost-of-service evidence (1.6x marginal cost from peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases), and cases like City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water District (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926 uphold tiered rates supported by real cost differentials, so a court may sever and preserve the cost-justified portion of the surcharge and the recycled-water debt-service allocation (30%), yielding partial rather than total invalidation.

The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is unlikely to succeed given Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, which treats parcel-delivered water charges as property-related fees subject to Prop 218, so that alternative theory does little to lower overall exposure.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water District (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
California Farm Bureau Federation v. State Water Resources Control Board (2011) 51 Cal.4th 421
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the gap between the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential and the $0.85 surcharge as a meaningful, severable 'incentive premium' (supporting partial invalidation) or would instead find the two roughly coextensive and defer to the district's rate-study judgment on the theory that peaking/drought-reserve costs alone justify a rate near the full differential — the hypothetical does not quantify how far $0.85 actually diverges from a pure 1.6x cost-based rate.
